title: Tries in Java
summary: Trie node structure, insert/search/startsWith, autocomplete, word search, and when to prefer Trie over HashMap.

questions.prompt: Each node in a Trie represents:
choices: A prefix — the path from root to this node spells the prefix
questions.explanation: The node itself doesn't store a character — the EDGE leading to it does. The node represents the prefix formed by that path.

questions.prompt: Trie insert time complexity for a word of length L:
choices: O(n) where n is total stored words
questions.explanation: Walk L edges, creating nodes where missing. Only L steps regardless of how many words exist.

questions.prompt: startsWith() differs from search() by:
choices: Not checking isEnd — just verifying the prefix path exists
questions.explanation: A prefix doesn't need to be a complete word — reaching the last prefix character node is sufficient.

questions.prompt: Insert 'cat'. search('ca') returns:
questions.code:
// Trie has only 'cat' inserted
// search checks isEnd at final node
questions.explanation: 'ca' path exists but the node at 'a' has isEnd=false — 'ca' was never inserted as a complete word.

questions.prompt: Insert 'cat'. startsWith('ca') returns:
questions.code:
// Trie has only 'cat'
// startsWith just needs the path
questions.explanation: The path c→a exists in the Trie — startsWith returns true without checking isEnd.

questions.prompt: Space complexity of a Trie storing n words of average length L:
questions.explanation: Each character of each word potentially needs a node — O(n×L) nodes total in the worst case (no shared prefixes).

questions.prompt: Space usage improves when stored words share:
choices: Common prefixes — shared prefix nodes are reused
questions.explanation: 'cat', 'car', 'cab' share the path c→a — that prefix is stored ONCE, not three times.

questions.prompt: Autocomplete starting from a prefix node uses:
choices: BFS to find the shortest words
DFS with backtracking to collect all words in the subtree
questions.explanation: DFS explores every branch; backtracking the StringBuilder undoes the character added for each branch.

questions.prompt: In the TrieNode[26] children array, the index for character 'c' is:
A Trie node with isEnd=true means:
choices: A complete word ends at this node
questions.explanation: isEnd=true marks that the prefix spelled by the root-to-this-node path is a stored word. Children may still exist (e.g. 'cat' and 'cats').

questions.prompt: Which operations is a Trie faster at than a HashSet<String>?
questions.explanation: Exact lookup is O(L) for both. Prefix check and autocomplete are O(L + output) for Trie vs O(n×L) scan for HashSet.

questions.prompt: Word Search II (find all dictionary words in a grid) uses Trie because:
choices: It makes DFS faster by pruning: branch abandoned when no word starts with current path
questions.explanation: Trie allows pruning — if the current board path is not a prefix of any dictionary word, stop immediately.

questions.prompt: Inserting 'app' into a Trie that already has 'apple':
choices: Reuses the existing a→p→p path, just marks isEnd=true at the second 'p' node
questions.explanation: The shared prefix is already there — only isEnd changes at the word boundary node.

questions.prompt: Trie sorted word iteration (DFS in children order 0→25) produces:
questions.explanation: Iterating children[0] to children[25] is alphabetical order — Trie gives sorted enumeration for free.

questions.prompt: Deleting a word from a Trie is complex because:
choices: You must not delete nodes that are shared by other words or are prefixes of other words
isEnd cannot be set to false
questions.explanation: Only remove the node if it has no children AND is not a prefix of another word — requires careful post-order DFS.

questions.prompt: HashMap<Character,TrieNode> vs TrieNode[26] for children:
choices: TrieNode[26] uses more memory but gives O(1) child access; HashMap saves space for sparse alphabets
questions.explanation: 26-element array is O(1) index. HashMap adds hashing overhead but uses less memory when alphabet is large or sparse.

questions.prompt: Trie with 'bear','bell','bid','bull','buy'. startsWith('b') returns:
questions.explanation: Path b exists — startsWith('b') is true. All stored words share the 'b' prefix.

questions.prompt: Longest common prefix of all words in a Trie is found by:
choices: Walking from root until a node branches (children > 1) or hits isEnd
Sorting words and comparing first and last
BFS level-order to the deepest level
questions.explanation: Follow the single-child chain from root — the moment a node has 2+ children or isEnd=true, the common prefix ends.

questions.prompt: A compressed Trie (Radix tree) reduces space by:
choices: Merging chains of single-child nodes into one edge label
questions.explanation: Instead of one node per character, a chain is compressed into one edge with a string label. Fewer nodes, same lookup time.

questions.prompt: After inserting 'cats' into a Trie that has 'cat', search('cat') returns:
choices: true — isEnd at 't' node is unchanged; 'cats' added a new 's' child
questions.explanation: Both words coexist: the 't' node stays isEnd=true, and a new 's' child is added below it.

flashcards.back: Walk path, create missing nodes. Set isEnd=true at last node. O(L).
search: reach end AND isEnd==true. startsWith: just reach end node.
'cat','car','cab' share c→a path — stored once. Space benefit of Trie.
Navigate to prefix node, then DFS with StringBuilder backtracking.
Prefix queries, autocomplete, spellcheck, word-search grid pruning.
DFS children[0..25] in order → words come out alphabetically.
isEnd=true AND has children = valid word that is also a prefix (e.g. 'cat' in 'cats').

project.brief:
Write TrieLab.java implementing a Trie class with:
1. insert(String word)
2. search(String word) → boolean
3. startsWith(String prefix) → boolean
4. autocomplete(String prefix) → List<String>  (sorted)
5. longestCommonPrefix(String[] words) → String
Include main() tests: prefix of another word ('cat'/'cats'), empty prefix autocomplete (all words), single character words.

checklist.text: insert correctly sets isEnd=true only at the final character node
search returns false for a prefix that was never inserted as a full word
startsWith returns true for any prefix of any inserted word
autocomplete uses DFS with StringBuilder backtracking — no duplicates
longestCommonPrefix walks the single-child chain until branch or isEnd

hints: autocomplete: pass 'new StringBuilder(prefix)' to DFS so the prefix is already in the builder.
longestCommonPrefix: insert all words, then walk from root following the only child at each step.
search vs startsWith: the ONLY code difference is 'return cur.isEnd' vs 'return true' at the end.
